Part B.key_evidence: The central candidate looks point-like in the science cutout, while the template does not show a clean stationary counterpart at exactly the same position; any nearby template flux appears offset. The difference cutout shows a compact residual rather than a broad host-like structure. Metadata also favor a one-off point source: classtar=1, fwhm=2.04, positive subtraction, ndethist=1 with very large ncovhist=1644, and deltajd=0. The nearest PS1 source is 7.08 arcsec away, so its high stellar sgscore is not a secure counterpart to the alert position.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with a real solar-system object (Asteroid). The required science-versus-template check for this very low-ndethist alert suggests an offset/new point source rather than fixed-position variability. A mover naturally explains the single spatially coincident detection despite extensive prior coverage, and there is no convincing host galaxy or nuclear association to support SN or AGN.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A subtraction artifact is the main alternative because the cutouts are noisy and the residual is not extremely prominent in this small stamp. A Galactic variable is less likely but possible if there is a very faint uncataloged star at the position. Still, the lack of a clear same-position reference source and ndethist=1 make the moving-object interpretation stronger.
